Mistrzostwa Swiata Juniorow W Biegu Na Orientacje

(The Junior World Orienteering Championships)
by The Brothers Bone

After warming up by traveling to the Arctic U.S. Champs and the Colorado 5+ Day, junior Cascade members Nicholas and Eric Bone (coincidentally, the authors of this account,) became full-fledged globetrotters by hopping the pond to hit the European O-circuit.

On the way to Poland, they had the misfortune of a five-and-a-half-hour layover in the most unpleasant airport terminal in the world—part of London's Heathrow airport. Defying conventional notions of comfort, Eric caught a nap amid the squalor of the departures "lounge." (Eric, a college junior, is accustomed to sleeping in crowded, messy places.) Following a four airplane, nine time zone trek (including a nearly two-hour delay in Warsaw) the Bones landed in Gdansk.

They scarcely had time to stand around looking American before they were greeted by team leader extraordinaire, Canadian Wil Smith, himself a recently retired junior. It was soon apparent by the convenience of the arrangements, that Wil had befriended the entire local orienteering population during the days prior to the Bones' arrival. As it turned out, he was also good friends with just about every competitor at the event, having met them at last year's Junior WOC in Italy.

Nick and Eric were personally escorted down Poland's smoothest motorway by the director of the school at which they were to stay, her startlingly anglophonic daughter, and Wil. Upon arrival at their place of accommodation—an unembellished concrete building, fondly christened School #16—the Bones threw off their bags and sat down to a special (late) session of dinner, served at Wil's request. Their bellies blessed with real food once again, they were taken to classroom #7, where they met the rest of the North American crew: Fellow U.S. junior hotshots, James Scarborough and Karen Williams; and the Canadian team, Mike Smith. They were joined a few days later by Matt Scott of Delaware Valley O.A., who arrived by train.

Classroom suite #7 was comfortably large, but was messy enough not to appear so. A web of clotheslines sagged with drying O-gear, making it look like the cleaners, but the maps lying about the room made it feel like home. The cots were infinitely more comfortable than airplane seats, and the weary travelers took full advantage of them.

The competition was still a few days away, so the intervening time was set aside for training, playing cards with the Irish team, passing the Frisbee among international circles, and getting accustomed to the new longitude. The group also devoted their efforts to surmounting the language barrier. Mike Smith made an important breakthrough in extra-lingual communication when he went to the food counter at lunch one day and indicated how much pasta he wanted by employing both hands to represent a large mound.

To their pleasant surprise, the brothers quickly became millionaires. A good thing, since their beverage of choice—woda naturalina niegazowana (or "bottled water")—was over five grand a liter. There are approximately 22,000 Polish zlotchy to 1 U.S. dollar. That sum will express mail two letters, buy a one person pizza with the works, or get you six(!) scoops of ice cream on a waffle cone.

The organizers of this Junior World Champs, the Polish navy sport club WKS Flota, made sure everybody knew it was a big deal by staging a parade through downtown Gdynia. The gloriously attired national teams were escorted on a three kilometer march by even more gloriously attired navy men carrying the countries' placards. The U.S. brought up the rear as Nicholas deftly wielded the stars and stripes,

making sure to brush the large flag across the tops of all heads within striking distance. The parade ended down a wide boulevard on the beautiful waterfront, where competitors were greeted and inspired by just about every longwinded Chairman and Vice Rear Admiral the navy could furnish. Several children had the honor of being photographed with the U.S. junior team. (Actually, it was the team members who felt honored.) The opening ceremony concluded with several numbers by WKS Flota's fine marching band, followed by music, then dancing (instigated by a drunken passer-by who wanted to share in the festive spirit.) Eric would have enjoyed the festivities much more if he hadn't thoughtfully kept so well hydrated that he desperately needed a urinal as soon as the parade began. He politely listened to all the excruciatingly comprehensive welcoming speeches (in Polish and English) before rushing off to the nearest water closet.

For the days of competition the athletes switched lodging to the event center, a much larger building (four stories and six wings) appropriately named School #41. It was there that they met the final member of their team, Eirik Moe—nominally a U.S. citizen but actually a Norweigan—and Dagfin, his pa'; and there that the ceremonial start draw was held to determine the start order for the three races.

The following day was the short distance race, with the semifinals in the morning, and the finals in the afternoon. The brothers, new to elite events and shaken by such high-class competition, made poor showings in the morning, placing into the "C" final (the top 48 placed into the "A," the next 48 into the "B," and the rest into the "C.") Matt Scott, destined to have mediocre runs for the entire three-day competition, also placed into the C final. James, Eirik, Karen, and Mike all qualified for the B. The afternoon went much better for Nicholas, who—despite his flimsy running background—finished just behind Matt in the middle of the C, and James, Eirik, and Mike all placed in the middle of the B race. But Karen had a less than satisfactory run, and poor Eric had it worse. Both finished near the bottom of their heats.

A welcome rest day followed, during which the party recuperated and received misinformation about the placement of the water stops for the classic distance race. The classic race for the men was 10.8 kilometers with 460 meters climb (really more like 560), which was especially grueling in the 38 degree C midday heat. Eric redeemed himself with the top North American finish—a very respectable 58th place—and the rest of the junior squad had decent though unspectacular runs. Wil, as always, was encouraging, and they all got a good night's sleep to prepare for the following day.

The relay courses, though neither much shorter nor much shallower than the classic, were softened somewhat by significantly cooler conditions and the knowledge that this was the last race. The fearless American leader had high hopes for the U.S. relay team—Eric, Matt and James—but the hoped-for miracles never occurred, and they managed only 38th place out of 50 teams. Nick, the third leg of the NAFTA goodwill team—Mike, Eirik and himself—unfortunately missed all the excitement, though he had a strong finish in the middle of the awards ceremony. Karen, also third leg, had the honor of forming a team with two Danes (their third member had broken an arm in the classic race).

Thus ended the competition, and after a long night of revelry and merrymaking (which the brothers were too wiped out to take part in very late), the travelers bid farewell to Poland and followed fate where it took them (to Norway, as it happened, but that's another story.)

More From The Tales Of Sacajawea

Elis Eberlein, President

By the time you read this, we should have tried a meet on our new color map at Horn Rapids County Park. It required a lot more work than I had anticipated, and it was rather hot out their some days. The biggest problem was really that the contours had to be totally redone from the USGS maps and that 1/3 of the area had 20' contours on the base-USGS map while the rest had 10' contours. The final product has 3 meter (really 10') contours. I was also lucky that a new print shop opened in N Richland just in time so that I could print it without leaving Tri-Cities.

All club members also received a flyer with the Fall schedule. I managed to make one important error in that flyer. It lists the date for the December meet as being on the 11th. That is WRONG!! The correct date is Saturday, December 10!!! I assume that listing causes some questions. We are going to have a meet in the morning. It will be at Horn Rapids, 10-noon, unless the weather turns totally bad, and we get a lot of snow. In that case we will have the meet on skies instead. So please confirm the meet schedule before you try to attend.

That same day in the afternoon, I scheduled two important events. First it is our first ever Annual Meeting. We have not had one yet, and there are a few things that needs to be formalized. We need to have some election of club official rather than people appointed by me at my own will, and we need to accept some kind of bylaws. Those are administrative details. More importantly, we need to discuss future local meet schedules, mapping projects, club economy, publicity, new member recruiting, and plans for future national meets.

As a carrot to attract members (other orienteers are very welcome as well) to attend the Annual Meeting, it will be followed by my selection of a Swedish Christmas Smorgsbord with herring (at least some), meat balls, ham, Ris a la Malta (dessert), glogg (a hot spicy wine drink), ginger snaps, and so on. The reason for this is my heritage in combination with the manger of the Orienteering sport in Sweden. I made it into a tradition

Page 3

when I lived in Los Angeles with the orienteering club there, and it grew into a quite popular event during those 3 years (if it can be called a tradition after 3 years?!). However, I need to know the number of people that plan to attend so I can cook an appropriate amount of food. So please let me know no later than December 6 that you are planning to attend. Hjertligt Velkomna!

St. Edward Park Midwinter Event

(formerly the Bog Slog)

We have renamed the "Bog Slog" with a more politically correct name. The Park has been concerned that the name "Bog Slog" implied that we were deliberately finding boggy areas to run through, to the environmental detriment of the Park, and has asked us to change the name of the event. So, come to the first (and probably last) St. Edward Park Midwinter Event (interim name only), prepared with a new (and PC) name for the event. We will have a contest during the after event festivities—with a prize of a fresh pie to the winner of the New Name Contest.

The St. Edward Park Midwinter Event will be held on Saturday, December 31 this year, with registration and starts from 10 to noon. St. Edward Park is off of Juanita Drive at about NE 160th NE. The park can be reached either by turning south on Juanita Drive from Bothell Way at Kenmore or from the NE 116th (turns into Juanita Drive) exit from I405. Watch for "O" signs, as the registration area may be at the Arrowhead School. Starts and finishes are still planned for the normal area by the pool.

Because of the New Years Eve holiday, the pool is closed and not available for rental. However, the usual seasonal festivities will take place at Debbie Newell's immediately following the meet (6216 NE 154th St., adjacent to the park, specific driving instructions available at the meet), with awards estimated at about 2 p.m.. This is a potluck, and everyone is invited. Because of New Year's eve, additional festivities are being planned for the evening. Information at the meet, or please check the hot-line.

Editorial Comment: Junior Team

Bruce McAlister

I enjoy orienteering; I think it is an outstanding sport and recreational activity, and for purely selfish reasons (more and better meets), I would like to see orienteering expand. Those of you who know me will recognize that I am not competitive, but one of those older, lost in the woods, recreational orienteers. In looking at other sports, and the slow growth of orienteering, I have become convinced, paradoxically, that one of the best ways to encourage the growth of recreational orienteering is to encourage the competitive aspect. Hence the soapbox. For better or worse, it is through competition that we will get the attention and exposure that we need.

Due to the efforts of a lot of people, we are off to an excellent start here in the Northwest, largely thanks to results from the WIOL program. Last spring Northwest clubs sponsored 11 students at the Intercollegiate Championships, this summer Eric and Nicholas Bone were at the World Junior Championships in Poland. Eric Riggers won his class at the US Champs in Anchorage. Ellen Boomer and Lisa Nordlund were at the World Student Championships in Switzerland. In October, 3 WIOL and 3 former WIOL participants attended the US relay championships in North Carolina. Eric Bone was second on the blue course the first day and first the second day. Fred Schmidt, from Jefferson was first on green with David Stainsby and Chris Hoyman just behind. And I apologize for those I haven't listed. Not only have all these orienteers started in WIOL, we've collected some great parents in the process.

With this terrific base to start from, I suggest we should deliberately encourage competitive, elite level orienteering here in the Northwest. In order to improve, we have to encourage our best junior orienteers to develop their orienteering skills, both by training programs, and by competition against the best.

So, what can we do? First, we can support and encourage WIOL. It takes a lot of effort to put on a series of weekly meets each winter, and Mike Schuh and Dave Tallent and Ardis Dull can use every volunteer they can get. Also, we should encourage as wide a high school participation as possible. We really need to get the high school cross-country coaches supporting this program. How many of you know some coaches you could contact? Another problem we have is that good meets and elite competition are hard to come by stuck out here in the corner of the country. It is hard to develop elite competitors just by running in park meets. The additional excitement and enthusiasm of the juniors attending national meets, and doing well has to be seen to be appreciated. One goal should be to develop a cadre of local orienteers who could challenge Eric and Debbie on any given day, so that at every meet we have would have E courses. We aren't there yet, but we are

Page 4

getting close. Now, if someone could just find a good job in Seattle for Martin Pardoe, and persuade the McKenzie boys that they should go to the UW!!!

But, there are things we can do. We can increase the possibilities for juniors to attend A meets out of the area, and international meets. We should support junior attendance at Canadian two-day events this spring, and at the US Intercollegiate Championships (Harriman State Park in April) and the relays in June, and hopefully we can get a couple of van loads to Colorado next summer. And we can encourage more A meets, or at least quality B meets in the Northwest. And who is going to do all the work? Why, naturally, all the new members we will attract.

This last year saw the Husky Club start a program of regular, daily training, combined with a series of Saturday training exercises. Bill Cusworth and Paul Magnus are doing this again. They need help and support (and more trainees).

I think we are on the threshold of an outstanding program, that, in a fairly short time, could be a "world-class" program (does anyone know if Ross Perot is an orienteer?). I don't think it at all beyond possibility to get the US Olympic Committee to take us seriously, and I think the benefits, in terms of expanded opportunities for all the rest of us "wandering in the woods for fun" orienteers would be tremendous.

If you think I'm wrong, beat me over the head (I've already taken some lumps), but if you think the program has potential, pitch right in and help. Mike and Dave and Ardis and the Husky Team can use all the support they can get. Or maybe you could drive some juniors to Canada or Colorado, or help with a frequent flyer ticket to a distant meet. At least cheer them on.

Convention Report

By Ardis Dull

The 1994 U.S. Orienteering Federation Convention was held August 16-19 at Cedar Glen Conference Center outside Toronto (yes, Canada). The following Seattle area orienteers attended portions of, if not all, activities: Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Dave Tallent, Bill Cusworth, Eric Bone, Nick Bone, Rick Hood, Ardis Dull. The Canadian Championships were held the preceding weekend, followed by the North American Champs the next. Short events were held on Tuesday & Thursday mornings. Some Canadians joined us at the convention, but not as many as hoped for.

The reason I've attended 6 straight conventions is that they're a unique opportunity to participate in a variety of workshops, fun activities & skill-building sessions, all the while informally meeting all those orienteers you may have only read about in O/NA. Workshops were offered on such topics as mapping, course design, A meet direction, training, older orienteers, permanent courses, club growth, junior orienteers, O in schools, scout programs, USOF, fun forms of O. A number of open forums were held, including one led by Rick Hood on land access. Interspersed during the days were physical activities—fun events such as vampire O, streamer O, team spectacular, volleyball & swimming, as well as training, score O & a 2-mile run. Evenings were filled with bonfires & socials by O/NA & K-Swiss.

The USOF AGM is held the last afternoon. This year there wasn't much controversy—although a lot of discussion on how best to use the anticipated substantial WOC93 profit. Rick Worner was elected President, and Per Stensby will receive the Silva Service Award.

Next years convention is planned for late June/early July in conjunction with the Colorado 5(+) Day. Hopefully the Northwest can continue to be well represented.

Want a Mileage-Plus Visa Card?

Would you like a Visa card where every dollar you charge gets you frequent flier mileage on United Air Lines? But it is not totally free. There is a hefty annual charge ($50 to $80 per year) depending on how many miles a year you fly, and the interest rates are high. But if you pay off your credit card each month, and run more than $3,000 to $4,000 per year through your account, they can be a good deal. If you don't want a card, but have some large cash purchases you can run through a card (e.g. tuition, auto, etc.), we could probably find a club card, run your expenses through the card and collect the miles for our junior program. If you would like an application (we get 5000 free miles per applicant), or like to charge a purchase, please see Bruce McAlister (821-6862). So far this year, Bruce has managed to get about 60,000 miles (3 free tickets) on United from credit purchases and bonuses. We need more.

Regional Representative Election

We are redoing the election of a Regional Representative for the USOF Board of Directors. The current regional representative is Bruce McAlister (Cascade) and the Alternate is Dan Ellsworth (Arctic). If there is someone who would like the job, Bruce would be happy to pass the job on, although he could serve as an alternate as he plans to continue to attend most Board meetings. The next Board meetings are scheduled for Florida in February, Intercollegiates (New York) at the end of April, the convention at Colorado in June and the US Champs (Southern Michigan) in the fall. There is no travel subsidy, but you get lots of mail.

This notice has already been sent to USOF members in Alaska and Oregon. Rather than a separate mailing, this is formal notice for USOF members in Washington. If you have any nominations, please send them to: Bruce McAlister, 14014 81st Pl. NE, Bothell, WA 98011, or email at 70673.1764@compuserve.com. Ballots will be sent out in December (assuming there are nominations), to take effect for the next Board meeting.

Field Notes

Rick Hood and Bruce McAlister

* In an effort to broader the "field notes" base, I would very much like to encourage anyone and everyone to send Peter Golde a postcard, voice mail, or e-mail with a field note whenever something strikes your fancy. Thus rather that having the field notes written by one person, we all will be able to contribute and hear the "latest word in the field". (As of next issue, I will be following this same tact an sending my field notes items to Peter.) So, join in the fun and contribute a little. Anything from a few words like, "I really liked..." to a couple of sentences is perfect.. Remember, if it doesn't fit on a postcard, it is too long for field notes.

* The first ever "A" meet in Florida is scheduled for February 18/19 at Orlando. This is also President's Day weekend, a Spring Break time for some eastern schools, and the Daytona 500 weekend. Both plane tickets and accommodations are getting hard to find. Some COC people are staying in park cabins, but these are going fast, too. If you are interested, act soon.

* Sammamish is taking an aggressive stance on membership; expect Mark and Wanda Howlett to contribute to the effort in 7 months or so, congratulations. (No doubt their son/daughter will be a strong contender for the WIOL Champ around 2013, given the quick speed, sound judgment, and good humor that runs in the family. I am not sure if this extends to King their dog, but I do know that he has a good sense of humor as he was more that willing to wear a few stickers that were "shared" with him by Anne at the Vampire O.)

* How popular were the award mugs at the Short Course and Ridge O events you ask? Well, according to Ardis Dull, who let her sister's son Eric use it once, he not only has claimed the mug, but won't drink his milk out of anything else. Does O blood run deep or what in the Pacific Northwest.

* Cascade new markers are a treat to reach—they really stand out. The club donated their faded, but still serviceable markers to the new club, Chuckanut Orienteering, that formed up in the Bellingham area, and that hosted their first meet, which was well received, at Washington State Park in this last month.

* It has been suggested that we have one regional meeting per year at whatever meet or event in the region will draw the greatest attendance. To provide a target, how about a short regional meeting at or following the Saturday night dinner at the Bullfrog Flats "A" Meet next August. What does everyone think? Write to Peter at Bearing 315.

* Congratulations to US (that is either us or U.S.). We retained the Pig War Trophy by a strategic defeat over Canada, in our yearly friendly competition. The event is really between Washington State Orienteers and those from the BC Orienteering Segment. I won't go into all the detail, which are being closely held for national security reasons, but look for next years Pig War Events to be part of more major events, thus promoting a higher turnout. PS—The ever inventive full Marti Anderson added to the pig war trophy appeal by making it a multimedia award with a little sound chip that play a catching tune.

* Regarding Cascade OC annual election for board members, as the nominating committee, I am pleased to say that all the serving members are willing to serve another term. However, if you are a club member and would like to vote for someone else, you may do so by calling me before the 29th of November at 367-2606 or at our "end-of-year celebration" described in the next field note.

* COC invites one and all to attend our end-of-year celebration to be held at COCO in Lake Forest Park, next to city hall on Ballinger Way and Lake City Way, this November 29th from 6:30 to 9ish. We have reserved the meeting room for this social event. No advanced sign up in required and you pay for whatever you order from the regular menus plus a 15% gratuity. We suggest those coming for dinner and wanting prime setting for Newell's 20 minute slide show through Orienteering Europe 1994 arrive between 6:30 and 7:00. Debbie will show her slide around 7:30. Then Nick and Eric Bone will share a few comments about their orienteering summer in Europe. At eight we will have the official election. At 8:02 Margie will say a few word and present COC's Presidential Award. And at 8:10 it is time for desert, conversation, etc. (Or at least that is the game plan.) This is not a COC only event and we hope to see SAMM, WIOL, NISQ, etc. there too. For more infor-

Page 6

mation give me a call at 367-2606. Lastly, we would like you to bring any O stories, maps, etc. of interest you have collected in 1994 as we will have a short "hear what was fun, funny, or flubbed" with awards going to the winners.

* Margie Bone, with just the slightest of compass strap twisting, the current COC president, has started an annual President's service award. She will make the selection (hey there should be some benefits with all the responsible that go with being president, at least until the pay increases) but invites one and all to call her with their input by the 25th of November at 634-2279. (The rule of thumb is that the person nominated should have contributed five consecutive years of service to the club and make a special contribution this year. These guidelines, just by chance, exclude Margie and myself from consideration.)

* The WIOL brochure is out; if you know of anyone who might like one, please call Mike Schuh, this year's director. As many know, in concert with the school league, we also host an adult competition know as the Winter Series. Of course, for those with more of a recreational approach, there is plenty of fun orienteering there for you as well. So take part in these two great "extended events" starting in mid-November and going through February.

* Several regions have implemented regional awards - similar to the Silva Award presented annually at the USOF Convention. The Silva award is for overall contribution to orienteering, not necessarily one particular event. Nominations could be made by anyone, selection would be made by a committee with non-regional representation, and the presentation made at the annual meeting (or some similar prestigious occasion). The Silva rules could just be adapted for our Region. Any thoughts? Write a letter to Bearing 315.

* Is anyone interested in mid-week cross country skiing over the Christmas - NewYears week in the Stevens Pass area? If there is enough interest, we may try to set a course in the Mill Creek area, or at Lake Wenatchee. Call Bruce (821-6962) or watch the hot-line.

* It was very nice to have Neil Philips, the President of the International Rogaine Federation, in town and at the after-Vampire O pizza gathering. Bob Reddick, the USOF Rogaine Chairman, hosted Neil, who was here for the US Geological Annual Conference. It was purely business when Bob to Neil to Mt. St. Helen for a tour of the area. As it happens the Rogaine meet site that Bob and Sammamish have proposed for their big 1997 event in planning in the shadow of the mountain. Speaking for those that got a chance to meet Neil, it was great having you visit Washington State and look for you seeing you and some of your countrymen here in at the big event. (FYI, the Sage Group is also planning to host another top notch Rogaine event in 1998.)

* Closer at hand, Sammamish has an A-Meet in the Cle Elum area (Bullfrog Flats, new map and all) Aug. 12/13, with everything on track. John Sincock is taking the lead. Also, in Kamloops July 8-11 (Logan Lake, new map as well) for three great days of A meet Orienteering at Sage Stomp 3 Day (Fri., Sat, & Tues.) The following weekend there is more A meet days east of Edmonton and the follow weekend even more just SW of Calgary. Together the Canadian package is called "Canada '95' and the phone number for information is 403 455-1916 with a fax of 403 453-8553. So what with the Colorado 5 plus day and Convention in the Rockies, there is a lot of super A meet opportunities with in a reasonable driving distance. (OK, a long drive.) Lastly, Sammamish and CROC are hosting a 24-hour Rogaine next summer in the White Pass general area. So, now is the time to ask Santa for those new O shoes or gaiters.

* Bill Cusworth and Bruce McAlister, by the time you read this, have returned from a planning meeting with the BC orienteers. As in past years (when Bruce or I went) the objective is to do everything possible to make in easy and enjoyable for us to attend their meets and visa-versa. (I am not sure who tried to jam the Washington Apple in the Pig's mouth, of Pig War Trophy fame, but for some reason it is now covered with maple syrup. Any volunteers to display/store the trophy this year?)

* Because it is such a short list, and because this is the last time I will be writing field notes in this form, I would like to gratefully acknowledge the staff our recent A event here. Courses: Peter Golde, designer/setter; Rick Breseman, vetter, Scott Donald, controller. Registration: Don Atkinson with help from Linda Hood and Karen MacDonald. Logo Design: Debbie Newell. Land Permission: Bruce McAlister. Map printing, bagging, sorting Peter, Don, and Rick. Saturday Starts: Margie Bone and the Siscel's. Saturday Finishes: Mike and Vivien Fritz, Robert and Sandy K. Miller, Bruce McAlister, Roger Sharp, Linda Hood. Posting and Announcing: Don Atkinson and Mike Schuh. Sunday Starts: Harvey Friedman, Dave Petty, and Larry Sorrenson. Sunday Finishes: Mike Schuh, Ardis Dull, Sandy K Miller, Andy Hill. Logo Design: Debbie Newell. Marker Pickup: Eric Bone and James Hopper, Bill Cusworth, Paul Magnus, Vladimer G., Wyatt R., Peter Golde, and more. Setting up and taking down: many unknown volunteers. Water, Awards, Camp Arrangements, etcing: Rick Hood. If I forgot your name, please remind me and I will make sure to print in even large typeface in the next issue, for without you the volunteers, we never would have been able to host this, or any event. If one can generalize from the meaningful and cheerful contributions of the above mentioned folks, the future of orienteering in the Pacific Northwest in indeed rich.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Thanks to the meets and all that participated for making this another classic. Look for, yes I know it is a product plug, VO5 next year to really make your hair stand on end.....Rick.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest November, 1994 Cascade Orienteering Club
Mistrzostwa Swiata Juniorow W Biegu Na Orientacje
(The Junior World Orienteering Championships) by The Brothers Bone

After warming up by traveling to the Arctic U.S. Champs and the Colorado 5+ Day, junior Cascade members Nicholas and Eric Bone (coincidentally, the authors of this account,) became full-fledged globetrotters by hopping the pond to hit the European O-circuit.

On the way to Poland, they had the misfortune of a five-and-a-half-hour layover in the most unpleasant airport terminal in the world—part of London's Heathrow airport. Defying conventional notions of comfort, Eric caught a nap amid the squalor of the departures "lounge." (Eric, a college junior, is accustomed to sleeping in crowded, messy places.) Following a four airplane, nine time zone trek (including a nearly two-hour delay in Warsaw) the Bones landed in Gdansk.

They scarcely had time to stand around looking American before they were greeted by team leader extraordinaire, Canadian Wil Smith, himself a recently retired junior. It was soon apparent by the convenience of the arrangements, that Wil had befriended the entire local orienteering population during the days prior to the Bones' arrival. As it turned out, he was also good friends with just about every competitor at the event, having met them at last year's Junior WOC in Italy.

Nick and Eric were personally escorted down Poland's smoothest motorway by the director of the school at which they were to stay, her startlingly anglophonic daughter, and Wil. Upon arrival at their place of accommodation—an unembellished concrete building, fondly christened School #16—the Bones threw off their bags and sat down to a special (late) session of dinner, served at Wil's request. Their bellies blessed with real food once again, they were taken to classroom #7, where they met the rest of the North American crew: Fellow U.S. junior hotshots, James Scarborough and Karen Williams; and the Canadian team, Mike Smith. They were joined a few days later by Matt Scott of Delaware Valley O.A., who arrived by train.

Classroom suite #7 was comfortably large, but was messy enough not to appear so. A web of clotheslines sagged with drying O-gear, making it look like the cleaners, but the maps lying about the room made it feel like home. The cots were infinitely more comfortable than airplane seats, and the weary travelers took full advantage of them.

The competition was still a few days away, so the intervening time was set aside for training, playing cards with the Irish team, passing the Frisbee among international circles, and getting accustomed to the new longitude. The group also devoted their efforts to surmounting the language barrier. Mike Smith made an important breakthrough in extra-lingual communication when he went to the food counter at lunch one day and indicated how much pasta he wanted by employing both hands to represent a large mound.

To their pleasant surprise, the brothers quickly became millionaires. A good thing, since their beverage of choice—woda naturalina niegazowana (or "bottled water")—was over five grand a liter. There are approximately 22,000 Polish zlotchy to 1 U.S. dollar. That sum will express mail two letters, buy a one person pizza with the works, or get you six(!) scoops of ice cream on a waffle cone.

The organizers of this Junior World Champs, the Polish navy sport club WKS Flota, made sure everybody knew it was a big deal by staging a parade through downtown Gdynia. The gloriously attired national teams were escorted on a three kilometer march by even more gloriously attired navy men carrying the countries' placards. The U.S. brought up the rear as Nicholas deftly wielded the stars and stripes,

making sure to brush the large flag across the tops of all heads within striking distance. The parade ended down a wide boulevard on the beautiful waterfront, where competitors were greeted and inspired by just about every longwinded Chairman and Vice Rear Admiral the navy could furnish. Several children had the honor of being photographed with the U.S. junior team. (Actually, it was the team members who felt honored.) The opening ceremony concluded with several numbers by WKS Flota's fine marching band, followed by music, then dancing (instigated by a drunken passer-by who wanted to share in the festive spirit.) Eric would have enjoyed the festivities much more if he hadn't thoughtfully kept so well hydrated that he desperately needed a urinal as soon as the parade began. He politely listened to all the excruciatingly comprehensive welcoming speeches (in Polish and English) before rushing off to the nearest water closet.

For the days of competition the athletes switched lodging to the event center, a much larger building (four stories and six wings) appropriately named School #41. It was there that they met the final member of their team, Eirik Moe—nominally a U.S. citizen but actually a Norweigan—and Dagfin, his pa'; and there that the ceremonial start draw was held to determine the start order for the three races.

The following day was the short distance race, with the semifinals in the morning, and the finals in the afternoon. The brothers, new to elite events and shaken by such high-class competition, made poor showings in the morning, placing into the "C" final (the top 48 placed into the "A," the next 48 into the "B," and the rest into the "C.") Matt Scott, destined to have mediocre runs for the entire three-day competition, also placed into the C final. James, Eirik, Karen, and Mike all qualified for the B. The afternoon went much better for Nicholas, who—despite his flimsy running background—finished just behind Matt in the middle of the C, and James, Eirik, and Mike all placed in the middle of the B race. But Karen had a less than satisfactory run, and poor Eric had it worse. Both finished near the bottom of their heats.

A welcome rest day followed, during which the party recuperated and received misinformation about the placement of the water stops for the classic distance race. The classic race for the men was 10.8 kilometers with 460 meters climb (really more like 560), which was especially grueling in the 38 degree C midday heat. Eric redeemed himself with the top North American finish—a very respectable 58th place—and the rest of the junior squad had decent though unspectacular runs. Wil, as always, was encouraging, and they all got a good night's sleep to prepare for the following day.

The relay courses, though neither much shorter nor much shallower than the classic, were softened somewhat by significantly cooler conditions and the knowledge that this was the last race. The fearless American leader had high hopes for the U.S. relay team—Eric, Matt and James—but the hoped-for miracles never occurred, and they managed only 38th place out of 50 teams. Nick, the third leg of the NAFTA goodwill team—Mike, Eirik and himself—unfortunately missed all the excitement, though he had a strong finish in the middle of the awards ceremony. Karen, also third leg, had the honor of forming a team with two Danes (their third member had broken an arm in the classic race).

Thus ended the competition, and after a long night of revelry and merrymaking (which the brothers were too wiped out to take part in very late), the travelers bid farewell to Poland and followed fate where it took them (to Norway, as it happened, but that's another story.)
More From The Tales Of Sacajawea

By the time you read this, we should have tried a meet on our new color map at Horn Rapids County Park. It required a lot more work than I had anticipated, and it was rather hot out their some days. The biggest problem was really that the contours had to be totally redone from the USGS maps and that 1/3 of the area had 20' contours on the base-USGS map while the rest had 10' contours. The final product has 3 meter (really 10') contours. I was also lucky that a new print shop opened in N Richland just in time so that I could print it without leaving Tri-Cities.

All club members also received a flyer with the Fall schedule. I managed to make one important error in that flyer. It lists the date for the December meet as being on the 11th. That is WRONG!! The correct date is Saturday, December 10!!! I assume that listing causes some questions. We are going to have a meet in the morning. It will be at Horn Rapids, 10-noon, unless the weather turns totally bad, and we get a lot of snow. In that case we will have the meet on skies instead. So please confirm the meet schedule before you try to attend.

That same day in the afternoon, I scheduled two important events. First it is our first ever Annual Meeting. We have not had one yet, and there are a few things that needs to be formalized. We need to have some election of club official rather than people appointed by me at my own will, and we need to accept some kind of bylaws. Those are administrative details. More importantly, we need to discuss future local meet schedules, mapping projects, club economy, publicity, new member recruiting, and plans for future national meets.

As a carrot to attract members (other orienteers are very welcome as well) to attend the Annual Meeting, it will be followed by my selection of a Swedish Christmas Smorgsbord with herring (at least some), meat balls, ham, Ris a la Malta (dessert), glogg (a hot spicy wine drink), ginger snaps, and so on. The reason for this is my heritage in combination with the manger of the Orienteering sport in Sweden. I made it into a tradition

when I lived in Los Angeles with the orienteering club there, and it grew into a quite popular event during those 3 years (if it can be called a tradition after 3 years?!). However, I need to know the number of people that plan to attend so I can cook an appropriate amount of food. So please let me know no later than December 6 that you are planning to attend. Hjertligt Velkomna!

We have renamed the "Bog Slog" with a more politically correct name. The Park has been concerned that the name "Bog Slog" implied that we were deliberately finding boggy areas to run through, to the environmental detriment of the Park, and has asked us to change the name of the event. So, come to the first (and probably last) St. Edward Park Midwinter Event (interim name only), prepared with a new (and PC) name for the event. We will have a contest during the after event festivities—with a prize of a fresh pie to the winner of the New Name Contest.

The St. Edward Park Midwinter Event will be held on Saturday, December 31 this year, with registration and starts from 10 to noon. St. Edward Park is off of Juanita Drive at about NE 160th NE. The park can be reached either by turning south on Juanita Drive from Bothell Way at Kenmore or from the NE 116th (turns into Juanita Drive) exit from I405. Watch for "O" signs, as the registration area may be at the Arrowhead School. Starts and finishes are still planned for the normal area by the pool.

Because of the New Years Eve holiday, the pool is closed and not available for rental. However, the usual seasonal festivities will take place at Debbie Newell's immediately following the meet (6216 NE 154th St., adjacent to the park, specific driving instructions available at the meet), with awards estimated at about 2 p.m.. This is a potluck, and everyone is invited. Because of New Year's eve, additional festivities are being planned for the evening. Information at the meet, or please check the hot-line.

I enjoy orienteering; I think it is an outstanding sport and recreational activity, and for purely selfish reasons (more and better meets), I would like to see orienteering expand. Those of you who know me will recognize that I am not competitive, but one of those older, lost in the woods, recreational orienteers. In looking at other sports, and the slow growth of orienteering, I have become convinced, paradoxically, that one of the best ways to encourage the growth of recreational orienteering is to encourage the competitive aspect. Hence the soapbox. For better or worse, it is through competition that we will get the attention and exposure that we need.

Due to the efforts of a lot of people, we are off to an excellent start here in the Northwest, largely thanks to results from the WIOL program. Last spring Northwest clubs sponsored 11 students at the Intercollegiate Championships, this summer Eric and Nicholas Bone were at the World Junior Championships in Poland. Eric Riggers won his class at the US Champs in Anchorage. Ellen Boomer and Lisa Nordlund were at the World Student Championships in Switzerland. In October, 3 WIOL and 3 former WIOL participants attended the US relay championships in North Carolina. Eric Bone was second on the blue course the first day and first the second day. Fred Schmidt, from Jefferson was first on green with David Stainsby and Chris Hoyman just behind. And I apologize for those I haven't listed. Not only have all these orienteers started in WIOL, we've collected some great parents in the process.

With this terrific base to start from, I suggest we should deliberately encourage competitive, elite level orienteering here in the Northwest. In order to improve, we have to encourage our best junior orienteers to develop their orienteering skills, both by training programs, and by competition against the best.

So, what can we do? First, we can support and encourage WIOL. It takes a lot of effort to put on a series of weekly meets each winter, and Mike Schuh and Dave Tallent and Ardis Dull can use every volunteer they can get. Also, we should encourage as wide a high school participation as possible. We really need to get the high school cross-country coaches supporting this program. How many of you know some coaches you could contact? Another problem we have is that good meets and elite competition are hard to come by stuck out here in the corner of the country. It is hard to develop elite competitors just by running in park meets. The additional excitement and enthusiasm of the juniors attending national meets, and doing well has to be seen to be appreciated. One goal should be to develop a cadre of local orienteers who could challenge Eric and Debbie on any given day, so that at every meet we have would have E courses. We aren't there yet, but we are
getting close. Now, if someone could just find a good job in Seattle for Martin Pardoe, and persuade the McKenzie boys that they should go to the UW!!!

But, there are things we can do. We can increase the possibilities for juniors to attend A meets out of the area, and international meets. We should support junior attendance at Canadian two-day events this spring, and at the US Intercollegiate Championships (Harriman State Park in April) and the relays in June, and hopefully we can get a couple of van loads to Colorado next summer. And we can encourage more A meets, or at least quality B meets in the Northwest. And who is going to do all the work? Why, naturally, all the new members we will attract.

This last year saw the Husky Club start a program of regular, daily training, combined with a series of Saturday training exercises. Bill Cusworth and Paul Magnus are doing this again. They need help and support (and more trainees).

I think we are on the threshold of an outstanding program, that, in a fairly short time, could be a "world-class" program (does anyone know if Ross Perot is an orienteer?). I don't think it at all beyond possibility to get the US Olympic Committee to take us seriously, and I think the benefits, in terms of expanded opportunities for all the rest of us "wandering in the woods for fun" orienteers would be tremendous.

If you think I'm wrong, beat me over the head (I've already taken some lumps), but if you think the program has potential, pitch right in and help. Mike and Dave and Ardis and the Husky Team can use all the support they can get. Or maybe you could drive some juniors to Canada or Colorado, or help with a frequent flyer ticket to a distant meet. At least cheer them on.

The 1994 U.S. Orienteering Federation Convention was held August 16-19 at Cedar Glen Conference Center outside Toronto (yes, Canada). The following Seattle area orienteers attended portions of, if not all, activities: Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Dave Tallent, Bill Cusworth, Eric Bone, Nick Bone, Rick Hood, Ardis Dull. The Canadian Championships were held the preceding weekend, followed by the North American Champs the next. Short events were held on Tuesday & Thursday mornings. Some Canadians joined us at the convention, but not as many as hoped for.

The reason I've attended 6 straight conventions is that they're a unique opportunity to participate in a variety of workshops, fun activities & skill-building sessions, all the while informally meeting all those orienteers you may have only read about in O/NA. Workshops were offered on such topics as mapping, course design, A meet direction, training, older orienteers, permanent courses, club growth, junior orienteers, O in schools, scout programs, USOF, fun forms of O. A number of open forums were held, including one led by Rick Hood on land access. Interspersed during the days were physical activities—fun events such as vampire O, streamer O, team spectacular, volleyball & swimming, as well as training, score O & a 2-mile run. Evenings were filled with bonfires & socials by O/NA & K-Swiss.

The USOF AGM is held the last afternoon. This year there wasn't much controversy—although a lot of discussion on how best to use the anticipated substantial WOC93 profit. Rick Worner was elected President, and Per Stensby will receive the Silva Service Award.
Next years convention is planned for late June/early July in conjunction with the Colorado 5(+) Day. Hopefully the Northwest can continue to be well represented.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
